original_abstract: As anthropogenic climate change poses an increasing threat to global health, the healthcare sector must urgently address its environmental footprint. Medical imaging is a carbon-intensive specialty, contributing significantly to global emissions through high energy consumption, single-use plastics, and vast data storage requirements. This article examines the primary sources of emissions within radiology—ranging from the idling states of MRI and CT scanners to the "Scope 3" supply chain impacts—and offers evidence-based solutions for improvement. By highlighting sustainable practices such as optimized patient scheduling, "digital temperance" in data archiving, and the adoption of circular economy models for equipment and waste, we aim to empower radiologists and imaging staff to lead meaningful ecological reform within their departments.
humanized_abstract: Overlapping pressures from anthropogenic climate change and global health security mean healthcare needs to act fast to address its footprint. Medical imaging, one of the carbon intenstive branches of medicine, is characterised by large amounts of energy used, single-use plastic products and an unprecedented amount of digital data produced. We review the major contributors in radiology such as the idling mode of MRI and CT machines and address "Scope 3" emissions related to the global supply chains that support radiology. Practical and pragmatic steps towards a greener radiology can be taken by all stakeholders in the form of smart patient positioning, "drinking moderate amounts of digital alcohol" and resource efficiency through the implementation of the principles of the circular economy into healthcare practices such as optimal use of existing medical imaging infrastructure and resource-efficient waste handling. The authors call upon radiologists and healthcare personnel to rise to the challenge and lead the way for sustainable change in the workplace.
